coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor enhanced by functionalized carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ measurement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Youquan
Huang, Xiaoyu
Xia, Binyun
Chen, Xiaohan
Jing, Xinyue
Wang, Ning
Li, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Precise detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) plays a critical role in maintaining biological homeostasis, ensuring industrial process quality control, and safeguarding ecological safety. Recently, various detection methods and sensors have been widely developed for measuring Fe3+, but the tradeoff between their performance and practical applications has not been achieved, such as high sensitivity, excellent specificity, simple structure and fabrication, low cost, and so on. In this study, an optical fiber SPR sensor was developed sensor for detecting Fe3+ based on the multimode-coreless-multimode (MNM) structured optical fiber. The refractive index (RI) sensitivity of the MNM fiber coated with a 60 nm Au film modified with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) reached 3440.45 nm/RIU. The organic groups functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and immobilized onto the Au substrates for specifically trapping Fe3+, which will cause the variation of the effective RI around the fiber, thereby resulting in the SPR wavelength shift in the transmission spectrum. By recording the red shift of the SPR wavelength, the amount of Fe3+ could be accurately quantified. Upon detection of Fe3+, the sensor was demonstrated with great linearity within a wide detection range of 10−12 to 10−2 M. The sensor’s sensitivity was obtained to be about 2.07 nm/lg (M) and its limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be about 2.88 × 10−13 M. It was also experimentally proved with excellent selectivity and stability, as well as the potential application in simulated real samples. The proposed sensing platform offered a simple configuration for Fe3+ detection with excellent performance and had various potential practical applications such as health monitoring, disease diagnosis, dietary planning, medication treatment monitoring, environmental monitoring, etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MNM structured optical fiber
Surface plasmon resonance
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Ferric ions

Fabrication of MNM structured optical fiber sensor
Thickness optimization and sensitization of the sensor
Optimization and sensitization of the sensor
Selectivity and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor was functionalized by AuNPs/CQDs. (a) Structure of the optical fiber Fe3+ sensor with AuNPs/CQDs and experimental device; (b) preparation of the sensor and loading mechanism of surface materials during the preparation.

(a) Spectra of optical fibers with different thicknesses in aqueous solution; (b) RI sensitivity of optical fibers with various thicknesses in salt solution; (c) the FOM for each thickness of the sensor; (d) RI sensitivity calibration curves of optical fibers in aqueous glycerol solution before and after functionalization of AuNPs.

(a) TEM image of CQDs; (b) High-resolution TEM images of CQDs; (c) SEM image of AuNPs and Au film on the optical fiber; (d) SEM image of the cross-section of the Au film coated on the optical fiber; (e) FTIR spectrum of CQDs; (f) full-range XPS survey spectrum; (g) FL emission spectra of the CQDs upon the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 0 to 10−2 M; (h) XPS survey spectra of the optical fiber SPR sensor functionalized with AuNPs/CQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+.

(a) The transmission spectrum of the optical fiber SPR sensor functionalized by AuNPs/CQDs; (b) the calibration curve of the wavelength shifts with Fe3+ concentration; (c) the wavelength shifts and response time of the optical fiber SPR sensor; (d) the selectivity of the optical fiber SPR sensor; (e) the calibration curve of the wavelength shifts in the simulated real sample. (f) the stability of the optical fiber SPR sensor.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive optical fiber SPR sensor enhanced by functionalized carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ measurement

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Precise detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) plays a critical role in maintaining biological homeostasis, ensuring industrial process quality control, and safeguarding ecological safety. Recently, various detection methods and sensors have been widely developed for measuring Fe3+, but the tradeoff between their performance and practical applications has not been achieved, such as high sensitivity, excellent specificity, simple structure and fabrication, low cost, and so on. In this study, an optical fiber SPR sensor was developed sensor for detecting Fe3+ based on the multimode-coreless-multimode (MNM) structured optical fiber. The refractive index (RI) sensitivity of the MNM fiber coated with a 60 nm Au film modified with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) reached 3440.45 nm/RIU. The organic groups functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and immobilized onto the Au substrates for specifically trapping Fe3+, which will cause the variation of the effective RI around the fiber, thereby resulting in the SPR wavelength shift in the transmission spectrum. By recording the red shift of the SPR wavelength, the amount of Fe3+ could be accurately quantified. Upon detection of Fe3+, the sensor was demonstrated with great linearity within a wide detection range of 10−12 to 10−2 M. The sensor’s sensitivity was obtained to be about 2.07 nm/lg (M) and its limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be about 2.88 × 10−13 M. It was also experimentally proved with excellent selectivity and stability, as well as the potential application in simulated real samples. The proposed sensing platform offered a simple configuration for Fe3+ detection with excellent performance and had various potential practical applications such as health monitoring, disease diagnosis, dietary planning, medication treatment monitoring, environmental monitoring, etc.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ions (Fe3+) play an indispensable role in various fields such as biological systems[1], ecosystems[2], and industrial production[3] as an essential trace element in the process of life. For instance, disruption of Fe³ ⁺ homeostasis in organisms can cause conditions like anemia, neurodegenerative disorders, or oxidative stress-induced damage[4]. As a limiting nutrient in ecosystems, Fe3+ deficiency or excess disrupts ecosystem stability[5]. In industrial production, Fe3+ concentration is a key parameter for assessing product quality (LiFePO4 cathodes, photovoltaic glass etc.)[6,7]. Therefore, developing a precise and interference-resistant Fe3+ detection method is critical for disease diagnosis, industrial process optimization, and environmental risk monitoring.

Traditional methods for detecting Fe3+ levels in organisms include spectrophotometry[8,9], atomic absorption spectrometry[10], and fluorescent probe[11,12], etc. However, most of these methods are still insufficient for real-time, portable, and rapid sensing. Therefore, it is imperative to develop new methods for Fe3+ detection with the merits of specific sensitivity, high selectivity, low reagent consumption, simple operation, and rapid analysis. Recently, optical fiber sensors have been widely used in the medical field due to their high sensitivity, high resolution, good chemical stability, and good biocompatibility[13] and a variety of optical fiber Fe3+ sensors have been developed by researchers. Yap et al[14]. reported a tapered optical fiber sensor functionalized with N, S-CQDs for the detection of Fe3+ in biological samples. Zhou et al[15]. reported a hydrogel optical fiber based on two types of quantum dots (QDs) doping for Fe3+ detection. Wang et al[16]. reported a novel optical fiber fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ based on AIEgens with a detection limit of 13.8 pM. Although much effort has been made in these studies, the performance of these sensors is still lacking in practical applications, such as low sensitivity and insufficient limit of detection (LOD). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop new optical fiber Fe3+ sensors with high sensitivity, specific selectivity, and low LOD.

Optical fiber surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors[17,18] have been widely used in biomonitoring because of their wide detection range, low sample requirements, good chemical stability, and biocompatibility. However, in the early stage of diseases, the concentration of pathogenic biomass is frequently low, and optical fiber SPR sensors also suffer from insufficient sensitivity. In recent years, two approaches have mainly been used to enhance the detection sensitivity of optical fiber SPR sensors[19]. On one hand, the performance of optical fiber sensors is ameliorated by modifying their structure. For instance, Liu et al[20]. designed a D-shaped optical fiber SPR sensor with a spectral sensitivity of 2000 ∼ 15,000 nm/RIU in the range of RI 1.22–1.33. However, optical fiber sensors with special structures are not only complicated to manufacture but are also particularly susceptible to interference from the external environment. Unlike these complexly structured optical fibers, the multimode-coreless-multimode (MNM) structured fiber[21] exists in sensor applications owing to a series of advantages, such as high sensitivity, simple structure, low cost, and ease of fabrication. On the other hand, SPR signal enhancement is achieved by modifying the sensitive material on the surface of the optical fiber SPR sensor, thereby increasing the sensitivity of the sensor. For instance, Zhao et al[22]. developed an optical fiber SPR sensor for pH measurements by cladding a hydrogel on the surface of an optical fiber, in which the number of free carboxylate ions in the hydrogel varied according to the pH value of the solution. Zheng et al[23]. modified P-mercaptophenylboronic acid (PMBA) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) on the surface of the sensor probe, and designed an optical fiber SPR sensor capable of detecting glucose and cholesterol concentrations. These methods enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of sensors by modifying the sensing materials on the surface of optical fibers. Among these sensitive materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)[24] have been widely used in optical fiber sensing owing to their unique optoelectronic properties[25,26], ease of synthesis, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. For instance, Nurul Hida Zainuddin et al[27]. explored an optical biosensor that utilizes tapered optical fiber integrated with post-deposition annealed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to improve Leptospira deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) detection. CQDs are nanomaterials with a large specific surface area[28,29] that can increase the number of binding sites for the sensor and thus improve the sensitivity of the sensor. In addition, a large number of functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [30], can be easily immobilized onto the CQDs during its synthesis to specifically capture the biochemical targets, which may improve the sensor performance.

In this work, an optical fiber SPR sensor with high sensitivity was designed for detecting Fe3+ of low concentration, which was simply fabricated by a multimode-coreless-multimode structured optical fiber coated with AuNPs modified Au film and subsequently immobilized with organic groups functionalized CQDs for specifically capturing Fe3+. By the merits of the SPR coupling effect from the Au film/AuNPs and the large specific area of CQDs, the sensitivity of sensor was improved. The optimal thickness of the Au film on the MNM structured optical fiber was explored to be about 60 nm, corresponding to an RI sensitivity of 3440.45 nm/RIU. When used for Fe3+ detection, the sensor exhibits excellent sensitivity of 2.07 nm/lg (M) and a LOD of 2.88 × 10−13 M, the detection performance of the sensor has a wider detection range and a lower LOD compared with most of the reported work[14–16]. Additionally, it was experimentally demonstrated with great linearity in a broad detection range along with good selectivity and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and working principle of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The design of the optical fiber Fe3+ sensor is shown in Fig. 1(a). The sensor utilizes a multimode-coreless-multimode (MMF-NCF-MMF) heterogeneous core construction, with the NCF component approximately 10 mm in length. The coreless ends were connected to the multimode fiber through a fusion splice. A thickness of approximately 60 nm Au film was then applied to the surface of the NCF portion of the fiber using a vacuum coater. The sensor was immersed in AuNPs solution so that the AuNPs with a diameter of approximately 20 nm were fixed on the surface of the Au film. AuNPs were able to significantly improve the sensitivity of the optical fiber sensor owing to their large specific surface area and LSPR effect. Finally, the CQDs were attached to the surface of the sensor (Au film and AuNPs) via chemical modification.

Fig. 1(a) illustrates the schematic diagram of the experimental device for the detection of Fe3+ using the functionalized optical fiber sensor. This setup primarily includes a white light source (HL-2000, Ocean Optics Inc., USA), optical fiber, spectrometer (Flame-T-VIS-NIR, Ocean Optics, USA), and a computer. Light emitted from the source travels through the multimode optical fiber to the sensor's sensing region, generating surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effects. Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) technology is a novel analytical technique based on optical principles[31]. An evanescent wave is generated when light undergoes total internal reflection at the metal surface. Resonance occurs when the frequency and wave number of the evanescent wave coincide with those of the surface plasmon wave induced by free electrons on the metal surface. This leads to the absorption of the incident light and a significant decrease in the energy of the reflected light. Consequently, a resonance peak forms in the reflection spectrum, with its position related to the RI of the medium adjacent to the metal film surface. Changes in the RI of the medium surrounding the sensor affect the propagation constant of the surface plasmon, altering the resonance between the evanescent wave and the surface plasmon wave. This change is detected by the spectrometer and represented on the computer as a shift in the SPR resonance peak. Thus, the spectral shift reflects changes in the RI of the environment. When AuNPs are modified on the surface of film coated optical fibers, localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects are generated, which are analogous to SPR effects[32–34]. The combination of the SPR effects generated by the Au film on the optical fiber sensor and the LSPR effects of AuNPs significantly enhances the sensor’s RI sensitivity.

When the sensing area of the sensor was submerged in an Fe3+ solution, the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs facilitated a coordination reaction with Fe3+. The mechanism is illustrated schematically in Fig. 1(b). The Fe3+ in the solution accumulates on the surface of the optical fiber sensor, causing a change in the sensor surface's RI and resulting in a shift of the SPR resonance peak. Different concentrations of Fe3+ correspond to varying SPR peak shifts, enabling specific detection of Fe3+.

Ethanol (C2H6O), chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2 H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7), glycerin and chlorine salts (FeCl3, ZnCl2, CdCl2, CrCl2, MnCl2, NH4Cl, LiCl, BaCl2, CoCl2, MgCl2, FeCl2, and NaCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). DL-Dithiothreitol (DTT, C4H10O2S2), sodium lignosulfonate (C20H24Na2O10S2), p-Phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), mercaptoacetic Acid (MAA, C2H4O2S), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, C8H17N3•HCl), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS, C4H5NO3) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Macklin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China), and stored at 4 °C until further use. Normal human serum was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. (Shanghai, China) and stored at −20 °C. Transferrin was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. (Shanghai, China) and stored at −4 °C. All experimental materials and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further treatment.

Coreless optical fibers (NCFs) (core/cladding diameter, 0/125 μm) and multimode fibers (MMFs) (core/cladding diameter, 62.5/125 μm) were purchased from Yangtze Optical Fiber and Cable Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

The morphologies of AuNPs on the optical fiber surface and the Au film on the optical fiber cross-section were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Gemini 300). The morphology and crystallization of the CQDs were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100 F). The elements contained in the CQDs and optical fiber sensors before and after testing for Fe3+ were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5000versaprobeIII). The FTIR spectra of the CQDs were obtained using FTIR (Nicolet iS 10), which was used to characterize the surface functional groups of the CQDs. The fluorescence excitation spectra of the CQDs and the different concentrations of Fe3+ The fluorescence excitation spectra of CQDs and mixed solutions of different concentrations of Fe3+ were tested by fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL, F-4500).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of MNM structured optical fiber sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The multimode-coreless-multimode (MNM) structured optical fiber sensor consisted of three sections of optical fibers, where the length of the coreless fiber was 10 mm, and multimode fibers at the two ends, which were connected to the light source and spectrometer, respectively. After removing the coating layer of the three optical fibers, they were connected by the fusion splicer (FSM-60s, Fujikura Ltd., Japan). A vacuum-coating machine was used to place a layer of Au film on the prepared MNM fiber-sensing section, and the thickness of the Au film was controlled by varying the evaporation time.

The CQDs were synthesized by the typical hydrothermal method[35,36]. First, 10 mg of sodium lignosulfonate and 20 mg of p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and then transferred to a 50 mL reaction kettle, which was placed in a 200 °C oven for 9 h. After cooling the reaction kettle to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm. The resulting suspensions containing CQDs were filtered through 0.22 μm filter membranes and then subjected to dialysis (1000 Da molecular weight cut-off) for approximately 72 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was evaporated and concentrated to obtain the desired CQDs solution.

The AuNPs were synthesized by the typical sodium citrate reduction method, which was the same as that in our previous work[37], the specific preparation procedures were as follows. First, 0.027 g chloroauric acid was dissolved in deionized (DI) water to obtain the solution with a concentration of 2.9 × 10−4 M. Second, 1.145 g sodium citrate was dissolved in DI water to obtain the solution with a concentration of 3.88 × 10−2 M solution. Both solutions were heated to 100 °C while being stirred. Then, 3 mL of the preheated sodium citrate solution was slowly injected into the preheated chloroauric acid solution. Subsequently, the solution was continuously heated and stirred for around 10 min until the color was transformed from yellow to burgundy, and the AuNPs solution was generated. Finally, the AuNPs solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm to eliminate the residual sodium citrate and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness optimization and sensitization of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The RI sensitivities of the MNM structured optical fiber sensors vary with varying Au film thicknesses. NaCl solutions of 0 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, 20 wt%, and 25 wt% were prepared to determine the optimal Au film thicknesses. The RI sensitivities of MNM structured optical fiber sensors with diverse Au film thicknesses were evaluated by recording the changes in surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectra. Subsequently, the impact of AuNPs on the RI sensitivity of these sensors was further investigated using a glycerol aqueous solution.

The AuNPs solution and CQDs solution were synthesized for spare. The modification process of the optical fiber Fe3+ sensor is shown in Fig. 1(b). The sensing region of the sensor was initially immersed in the DTT (0.5 M, 1 mL) solution for 1.5 h, DTT connected AuNPs to the Au film on the surface of the optical fiber through Au-S bonds. The sensor was subsequently immersed in the prepared AuNPs solution for 3 h. The sensor was removed and air-dried. AuNPs with unique optical properties and a large specific surface area can effectively improve the sensitivity of the sensor. The coreless segment of the optical fiber Fe3+ sensor was subsequently immersed in the MAA (50 mM) solution overnight to generate carboxyl groups on the sensor's surface. Subsequently, the optical fiber was immersed in a mixture of EDC (0.2 mM, PBS solution, pH= 5.5) and NHS (0.5 mM, PBS solution, pH= 5.5) for 5 h as a way to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of the optical fiber. Subsequently, the optical fiber was immersed in the CQDs solution for 3 h. The amino groups on the CQDs were coupled with the activated carboxyl groups to successfully connect the CQDs to the sensor surface[38,39]. Finally, the Au film-coated sensing area was washed three times with PBS to eliminate unbound CQDs and other residues.

Standard buffered Fe3+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 10−12 M to 10−2 M in step 102 were prepared by using PBS. For optical fiber Fe3+ sensor sensitivity measurements, the optical fiber sensing area was immersed in different concentrations of Fe3+ solution for 40 min, and the sensor was rinsed clean with PBS at the end of each measurement. All experiments were performed at a temperature of 26 ± 0.1 ℃. Under the same experimental conditions, the following metal ions (Zn2+, Cd2+, Li+, Cr2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Na+ ions) were selected as interfering ions to study the selectivity of the sensors, which were evaluated based on the amount of spectral shift of the optical fiber spectra at an ion concentration of 10−4 M selectivity. Furthermore, the interference of common competitive ligands prevalent in the physiological environment, namely transferrin (TRF) and citric acid (CA), on Fe3+ detection was taken into account. To assess selectivity of the sensor, its responses to 10−4 M CA and 10 mg/L TRF were examined. The performance of the sensor for Fe3+ detection in a complex environment was investigated by adding different concentrations of Fe3+ to 1000-fold diluted human serum under consistent experimental conditions. The stability of the sensor probes was evaluated by immersing them in different concentrations (10−12, 10−8, 10−4 M) of Fe3+ solutions for 6 h under identical experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and sensitization of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of multimode-coreless-multimode structured SPR fibers is influenced by multiple factors, and the Au film thickness is a crucial one. To ascertain the optimal Au film thickness for the sensor, refractive index (RI) sensitivity tests were conducted on optical fibers with diverse Au film thicknesses. Fig. 2 presents the results of the RI sensitivity tests for MNM structured optical fibers with varying Au film thicknesses, along with the gold nanoparticle sensitization effect. Fig. 2(a) shows that the spectra corresponding to different Au film thicknesses exhibit distinct peak shapes, and the optimal peak shape is obtained when the Au film thickness is 59.7 nm. Fig. 2(b) and (c) depict the variation in the RI sensitivity of the MNM structured optical fiber sensor as the Au film thickness changes. The error bars in Fig. 2(b) were obtained by repeating the experiment for 3 times by using 3 sensor probes. The RI sensitivity of the optical fiber initially raised and subsequently declined as the thickness of the Au film increased, reaching its peak at around 60 nm.

The key parameter of an optical fiber sensor is sensitivity, which commonly correlates with the sensor's structure and surface coating. To further explore the impact of Au film thickness on sensor performance, the relationship between the fiber sensor's quality factor (FOM) and Au film thickness was examined[40].

The FOM was defined as:
(1)
FOM =
S FWHM
× VD
Where S is the sensor sensitivity, FWHM is the full width of the corresponding half peak of the SPR spectra, and VD is defined as the difference in transmittance between the maxima and minima of the corresponding valleys. Fig. 2(c) shows the FOM corresponding to the optical fibers with different Au film thicknesses, from which it can be seen that the optimal FOM of the optical fiber sensor is reached at an Au film thickness of approximately 59.7 nm. As the Au film thickness increased, the FOM of the optical fiber sensor initially increased, peaked at 59.7 nm, and subsequently decreased.

To ascertain the sensitizing effect of AuNPs on MNM structured optical fiber sensors, we tested the RI sensitivity of three optical fibers before and after the modification with AuNPs in aqueous solution of glycerol. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the results indicate that the sensitivity of the modified fiber increased by 34.32 %, reaching 3440.45 nm/RIU.

Fig. 3(a) presents a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CQDs. It is evident that the synthesized CQDs are spherical nanoparticles with a uniform particle distribution and no significant agglomeration. Statistical analysis of the CQDs’ particle size within the TEM image region reveals a dynamic size distribution, with an approximate size of 2 nm. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image indicates that the CQDs possess good crystallinity, exhibiting a lattice spacing of around 0.21 nm. Fig. 3(c) presents a SEM image of AuNPs, evidently, a layer of lustrous spherical nanoparticles is observable on the optical fiber surface. These nanoparticles exhibit a single spherical morphology and are uniformly dispersed across the Au film surface. This finding implied that the AuNPs have been successfully modified onto the surface of the film coated optical fiber. Meanwhile, Fig. 3(d) depicts the cross-sectional SEM image of the film coated fiber, from which it is distinctly apparent that the Au film adheres closely to the optical fiber surface, with an approximate thickness of 59.7 nm.

To determine the surface functional groups of CQDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted, as illustrated in Fig. 3(e). The presence of O-H and N-H bonds was confirmed by peaks located at 3100–3500 cm−1 [41]. In addition, the FTIR spectra of CQDs at 2900, 1630, 1500, and 1380 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic absorption peaks of C–H, CO, CC, and C–N, respectively[36], providing further evidence for the elemental composition and surface groups of CQDs.

The high-resolution O1s, N1s, and C1s XPS spectra of the CQDs are presented in Fig. 3(f). The results further corroborate that the synthesized CQDs predominantly consist of C, N, and O elements[42]. In light of the FTIR and XPS results, the presence of functional groups, namely carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, on the CQDs’ surface is ascertained[43,44]. This finding furnishes robust evidence that CQDs can be surface-modified onto the optical fiber sensor.

Fig. 3(g) presents the FL emission spectra of the CQDs solutions with Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 10−12 to 10−2 M. As the Fe3+ concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs decreased. When the Fe3+ concentration reached 10−2 M, the fluorescence of the CQDs completely disappeared, indicating that the CQDs were fully bound to Fe3+. Fig. 3(h) shows the XPS spectra of the AuNPs/CQDs functionalized optical fiber sensor before and after the detection of Fe3+. The newly emerged Fe2p peak at 710 eV suggested that Fe3+ was successfully attached to the CQDs[45]. Consequently, the AuNPs/CQDs functionalized optical fiber SPR sensor was capable of effectively detecting Fe3+ in solution.

The effectiveness of the SPR sensor for the detection of Fe3+ was examined, and the performance of the sensor was evaluated by measuring different concentrations of Fe3+ solution using the prepared sensor while maintaining the consistency of other experimental variables. The wavelength variation of the transmission spectrum reflects the ability of the sensor for the detection of Fe3+.

Fig. 4(a) shows the response of the MNM structured optical fiber SPR sensor to different concentrations of Fe3+, and the result shows that rapid on-line detection of Fe3+ can be achieved in the Fe3+ concentration range of 10−12 M to 10−2 M. The response of the sensor to Fe3+ was rapid and significant, and the wavelength shift (Δλ) of the transmission spectrum of the SPR sensor gradually became larger as the concentration of Fe3+ increased in the range of 10−12 M to 10−2 M. Fig. 4(b) presents the calibration curves of wavelength shift and Fe3+ concentration of the sensor under five experiments with the same conditions, and the results show that the wavelength shift has a favorable linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration in the range of Fe3+ concentration from 10−12 to 10−2 M. The average wavelength shifts of the sensor reached 2.38 nm for a Fe3+ concentration of 10−12 M, and 22.08 nm for a Fe3+ concentration of 10−2 M. These results show that the sensor has sufficient sensitivity and is capable of detecting Fe3+.

Response time constitutes a significant metric for assessing the performance of a sensor. It reflects both the sensitivity and the response speed of the sensor to environmental alterations. Generally, the response time of an optical fiber SPR sensor pertains to the duration from the commencement of detection until the spectral signal attains stability. Fig. 4(c) presents the gradient plot of the spectral wavelength shift of the sensor against time. It can be discerned therefrom that the wavelength shift of the sensor under each concentration stabilizes in approximately 30 min, signifying that the response time of the sensor is around 30 min. This response time implies that the sensor is capable of effecting online detection and furnishing a rapid response to Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor pertains to its capacity to detect Fe3+ devoid of interference from other environmental constituents. For the evaluation of the sensor's selectivity, standard solutions of metal ions, namely Cd2+, Zn2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Li+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Na+, and NH₄+, and two common competitive ligands (transferrin and citric acid) were dissolved in PBS buffer, serving as potential interfering agents. Among them, the concentration of TRF is 10 mg/L, and the concentration of other interfering substances is uniformly 10−4 M. The experimental results are presented in Fig. 4(d). The results evince that the SPR sensor manifests the most pronounced response to Fe3+, with a resonance wavelength shift of 17.85 nm, whereas the responses to other ions remain within 1.6 nm, the responses to TRF and CA remain within 2 nm, thereby attesting to the sensor's outstanding selectivity. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the CQDs' surface generated a complexation reaction with Fe3+, efficaciously immobilizing Fe3+ on the optical fiber's surface and, consequently, realizing specificity in the detection of Fe3+.

To further validate the sensor's capacity to detect Fe3+ within complex settings, simulated samples with diverse Fe3+ concentrations were prepared using diluted normal human serum. The detection results are shown in Fig. 4(e), the sensitivity of sensor was obtained to be about 1.81 nm/lg (M). The result signified that the optical fiber sensor demonstrates outstanding sensitivity when detecting Fe3+ in a normal human serum milieu. The aforementioned experiments attested that the AuNPs/CQDs functionalized optical fiber sensors perform well even under complex circumstances, guaranteeing reliable Fe3+ detection in practical applications.

Stability is a critical parameter for the sensor in practical applications, defined as its ability to consistently detect the target substance across various environments. To evaluate the stability of sensors, five MNM structured sensors were immersed in Fe3+ solutions with varying concentrations (10−4 M, 10−8 M, and 10−12 M) for 6 h under identical experimental conditions. Fig. 4(f) illustrates the fluctuations of the sensor transmission spectra measured at half an hour interval. The maximum shifts of the resonance wavelength over 6 h were 0.5 nm, 0.3 nm, and 0.15 nm at Fe³ ⁺ concentrations of 10−4 M, 10−8 M, and 10−12 M, respectively.

The LOD refers to the lowest signal level that a sensor can effectively detect and is a critical parameter for evaluating the sensor's performance and suitability, alongside selectivity, repeatability, and stability.

The following is the formula for calculating the limit of detection:
(2)
LOD =
3
σ blank
S surf
Where
σ
blank
is the standard deviation of the blank point and S
Surf is the surface sensitivity. Five sensor probes were placed in the PBS solution for detection. After calculating the value of
σ
blank
(found to be 0.16), it was substituted into Eq. (2) to calculate the sensor's LOD as 2.88 × 10−13 M. The SPR sensor developed in this study exhibits a linear response to Fe3+ concentration changes, featuring a low LOD, high specific selectivity, and excellent stability.

As shown in Table 1, we summarized some other Fe3+ detection methods and compared their performance with the SPR sensor. Most of the conventional fluorescence methods obtained poor LOD in the range of 10−4 M to 10−9 M[46,47]. The fluorescent fiber sensor is a combination of fluorescence detection and optical fiber technology, and the use of a fluorescent fiber sensor for Fe3+ detection greatly simplifies the detection process[15,16,48,49], but the performance is not greatly improved compared with traditional fluorescence methods, and the LOD is only reduced by one order of magnitude compared with traditional fluorescence methods. In comparison to fluorescence methods, particularly fluorescent optical fiber sensors, the sensor we designed offers a wider detection range and a lower LOD. Additionally, they achieved the detection of iron ion concentration by recording the changes in weak fluorescence intensity, which was easily affected by light sources and the external environment. Optical chemical sensors utilizing electrostatic spinning technology have extremely low LOD and fast response times for Fe3+, but are limited by specific environments and harsh detection conditions[50]. In contrast, SPR sensors exhibit good selectivity and can specifically detect Fe3+ in complex environments. Compared with interferometric optical fiber sensors[14], the sensor with a simpler configuration has a wider detection range and lower LOD. R. Fu et al[51]. designed an optical fiber SPR sensor using Ag film as the sensitive material based on the redox reaction between Ag and Fe3+, which was proved with a wide detection range and low LOD for Fe3+ detection. However, the sensor was also demonstrated with a wider detection range and lower LOD. More importantly, the application of their sensor is limited because the Ag film is easily oxidized in air, restricting its use in specific situations. In contrast, the sensor utilizes carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Au substrates as the sensitive material, offering good biochemical stability, biocompatibility, and excellent selectivity for Fe3+, as well as significant potential for practical applications.

However, the sensor proposed here suffers from the disadvantage of a long detection time of 30 min. In practical applications, the response time may be effectively reduced by pretreating the test sample or changing the sensitive probes. Additionally, the motion and enrichment of the detection target around the optical fiber sensor can be enhanced by applying external electric, magnetic, or acoustic fields, as well as chemicals or catalysts, which may further shorten the response time.

In summary, an optical fiber SPR sensor was successfully fabricated based on a simple and low-cost MNM structured optical fiber for detection of Fe3+ qualitatively and quantitatively. The sensor is coated with Au nanofilm modified with AuNPs to enhance its RI sensitivity by merits of the SPR coupling effect and the organic groups functionalized CQDs were immobilized on the fiber for specific detection of Fe3+. The sensitivity of sensor for Fe3+ was experimentally determined to be approximately 2.07 nm/lg (M) over the concentration range of 10−12 M to 10−2 M, with the LOD of 2.88 × 10−13 M. Additionally, the sensor also exhibits great selectivity and stability, making it suitable for Fe3+ detection in complex environments. The proposed sensing platform can be adapted to various other biochemical targets and may serve as a promising solution for practical applications in strategic fields such as biomedicine, environmental monitoring, and energy.

Jing Xinyue: Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Jinhua: Resources. Xia Binyun: Data curation, Conceptualization. Wang Ning: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhou Youquan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chen Xiaohan: Data curation, Conceptualization. Huang Xiaoyu: Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (Grant N0.2022YFB2404300); The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.62375208); The Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan -Basic Research (Grant No.2023010201010095) and Hubei Optical Fundamental Research Center.
